paragraphs.text: The fate of Harry and Meghan, the Duke and Duchess of Sussex, continues to arrest public attention. It does not tend to be generated from universal sympathy for the couple, nor from the widespread criticism they provoke. Rather, many across the globe are intrigued by whether they will continue to thrive or merely survive.

The debate between their admirers and critics seems to focus on the endgame. Divorce, debt and disaster are constant themes about the Sussexes’ future. Many gloat that Harry is no longer the Royal Family’s ‘spare’ but has become Meghan’s. Indeed, in an interview for Harper’s Bazaar’s Christmas 2025 issue, Meghan appeared to revel in Harry’s puppy love for her.

Ever since they moved to California in 2020, many have speculated that the Sussexes’ marriage was doomed. Harry, it was said, would fall out of love with the sea and sunshine and pine for his British roots – his family and the friends he left behind. Others predicted that Meghan would become bored with the Prince and even entice a Silicon Valley billionaire to provide the wealth and status she craves. Current evidence suggests that the doom-mongers are wrong – so far. But every week the Sussexes’ antics provoke speculation.
Their narrative is propelled by their incessant search for publicity. Hardly a day passes without Meghan posting photos, videos and

Interspersed between Meghan’s carefully orchestrated appearances, Harry has increasingly struggled to protect his princely status. With nothing overtly original to say, he has steadfastly refused to acknowledge the harm caused by Spare, his bestselling autobiography, and his subversive TV interviews. Trading on his birthright, he is heralded when promoting a charity but criticised when he uses his name to earn an extremely handsome fee.

Five years ago, Revenge, my book about the Sussexes’ war against the Windsors, exposed the truth about Meghan’s past. It revealed how she had ditched her family, friends and others to become famous. Simultaneously, the book traced her manipulation of past events to rewrite her history. The climax of her ‘truth’ was her interview with Oprah Winfrey in 2021. Since then, she has appeared to separate herself from Harry except when a joint appearance would enrich her publicity. Meghan’s self-promotion as Her Royal Highness, the Duchess of Sussex, has become more sophisticated. Entrenched in California, her bid for commercial success relies on her ‘truth’, which others would say bears little resemblance to objective facts. However, no humiliation or criticism has crippled her breathtaking ambition.
At the same time, Harry’s troubles have mounted. Isolated from Britain, his charitable activities have become threadbare and his

With the Royal Family weakened by scandal and sickness, the Sussexes and the British monarchy are at a crossroads. The dramatic events of the past five years and new disclosures about details of their lives before ‘Megxit’ justify a new book with a fresh and penetrative look at Meghan, Harry and the Royal Family. In many ways, 2026 will be a make-or-break year for them all.
The book opens during the momentous week of Queen Elizabeth II’s death in September 2022.

In the countdown to the big moment, Ella Robertson read Meghan Markle’s proposed speech on the Autocue. ‘She’s fucked it,’ groaned Robertson. Instead of speaking as agreed about the charity One Young World, Meghan’s speech was focused on herself. Nearly fifty times in just ten minutes, she intended to say ‘I’.

On 5th September 2022, Meghan was due to address 2,300 people for One Young World’s conference in Manchester. Founded in 2010 in London, the organisation brought young leaders together every year in a different country to discuss global challenges.

The co-founder, Kate Robertson, and her daughter Ella had been nervous about inviting Meghan to Manchester. ‘Megxit’ was still a hotly emotional topic – Harry and Meghan had only left the UK in March 2020 – and the Sussexes’ anticipated return to Britain from California had excited intense speculation. Buckingham Palace had disclosed that the Queen would not meet her grandson.

Despite the snubs, the Sussexes had conjured a short itinerary of three events headlined ‘Compassion in Action’. First, they would appear in Manchester, then dash to Düsseldorf to promote the 2023 Invictus Games before returning to London for Harry to host the WellChild awards. Given recent explosive events, the Sussexes’ appearances were guaranteed publicity.

Despite the widespread anger among older Britons towards Harry and Meghan, many young people hailed the couple as heroes. Among those younger factions their popularity was undeniably magnetic. Meghan, they believed, was the victim of the racist royals, and that undoubtedly Harry had been abused by his father and brother. A new generation of aspiring young leaders, the Robertsons had decided, would welcome Meghan in Manchester. ‘They were the most attention-grabbing couple in the world,’ judged Ella. ‘No one else could get so much attention for an event.’
Yet the Robertsons had good reason to be fearful.

During endless conversations and emails with the Sussexes’ staff in California, Ella Robertson had sought guarantees that there would be no ‘explosions’ to distract from the conference. Ever since Meghan’s interview with Oprah Winfrey in 2021, everything she said had become sensational. Although sensitive to criticism, Meghan was proud of the Oprah interview, not least because the reward for denouncing the Royal Family was renewed global stardom. Robertson sought reassurances from James Holt, the Sussexes’ chief of staff. ‘Nothing’s coming up that I’m aware of,’ he affirmed. Holt evidently forgot to mention that Meghan would be hosting a series of podcasts and the imminent publication of an interview with The Cut, a New York magazine. Holt’s priority was to fuel Meghan’s celebrity, especially as the champion of humanitarian causes.

Appropriately, Meghan had agreed that her short speech in Manchester would focus on empowering women and gender equality. Pointedly, she was asked not to speak about herself and not to wear expensive clothes. From past experience, the Robertsons had reasons to be nervous.

During her rise to stardom, Meghan had previously worked with One Young World and it clearly helped to boost her celebrity. Although the actress was largely unknown in 2014, Kate Robertson had been persuaded by the photographer Misan Harriman to give Meghan a slot at the Dublin conference. ‘Choose her. She’ll be a star,’ urged Harriman. Not only because she was acting in Suits, the American cable TV series, explained Harriman, but because ‘she’s a hustler and a climber. She’s looking for ins and ups.’

Two years later, in September 2016, Meghan was booked to appear at the One Young World conference in Ottawa. By then, she had been secretly dating Harry for three months. Her ambition, she had confessed to her publicist Gina Nelthorpe-Cowne, was to marry the Prince. ‘With Harry by my side,’ she had whispered at a London restaurant, ‘we can change the world.’

During those action-packed two days in Ottawa, the Robertsons and Nelthorpe-Cowne noticed how Meghan had altered from just two years previously. Forthrightly, she demanded to feature in the official photographs with the Canadian prime minister Justin Trudeau, UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan and Bob Geldof. She also demanded equivalence with the actress Emma Watson. She had inveigled her way into Vanity Fair’s photograph featuring ‘exceptional young leaders’ and had scrutinised whether her name featured as a ‘Very Very Important Person’ (VVIP) on the roster. Disgruntled by her failure to secure Suits set in Toronto and departed without making her listed speech to the conference. Nelthorpe-Cowne was left tearful. With a move that could seem heartless towards those unable to further her ambitions, it was becoming apparent that Meghan would allow no one to forget that she was to become one of the world’s most famous women.

Despite that disruption, Ella Robertson could not resist again inviting Meghan to address 2,000 young leaders at the One Young World conference in London’s Royal Albert Hall on 22nd October 2019. Now the newly married Duchess was certain to be a star attraction. Un-surprisingly, the negotiations were complicated. Jason Knauf, Meghan’s young American-born Palace press secretary, was emphatic: ‘Meghan demands that you big up her humanitarian credentials.’ Calls to confirm Meghan’s presence were answered ‘yes’ by Kensington Palace and then followed up by uncertainty.

‘It’s a vipers’ nest in her office,’ complained Ella Robertson. Every request to confirm Meghan’s participation produced conflicting replies from her assistants – positive from Amy Pickerill; frosty from Beth Herlihy; encouraging from Sara Latham, a Kensington Palace media official who replied, ‘We want to do the project and we want to do it now’; and finally hectic from Samantha Cohen, the Sussexes’ most senior advisor. ‘It became a nightmare to get her to the event,’ recalled Robertson.

The chaos had spread to the London headquarters of the Invictus Games, Harry’s successful sports competition for wounded military veterans. ‘It’s a shit show over there,’ chief executive Don Reid reported about Kensington Palace. ‘Harry has no idea what’s happening.’ The nub was a breakdown of relations between the Sussexes and their staff – and everyone knew the reason.
Finally, Meghan agreed to appear at the Royal Albert Hall. In return, One Young World funded a £200,000 scholarship for Meghan to mention in her speech.

On meeting Meghan in Windsor Castle for the discussion about her favourite topic – feminism – Ella Robertson was surprised. Five months after Archie’s birth, ‘She was traumatised, shell-shocked and brittle.’ Nevertheless, Meghan behaved professionally. Well prepared, she introduced her ‘surprise guest’ to the panel – Harry. He had just finalised the membership of Britain’s team to compete at the following year’s Invictus Games in the Hague. With Harry in a supporting role, the BBC’s film featured the Sussexes as genuine stars.

By then, gossip about the confusion and arguments among the Sussexes and Cambridges – the so-called ‘Fab Four’ – in Kensington Palace and beyond had seeped into the media. By now dubbed ‘Duchess Difficult’, Meghan was wilting under the pressure.

Meghan’s plight attracted sympathy in Westminster. Seventy-two female MPs expressed their ‘solidarity’ with her against the media and, implicitly, the Palace. Simultaneously, American politicians including Hillary Clinton and Michelle Obama hailed Meghan as a hero. With targeted briefings, Meghan was successfully building a narrative about her suffering. Like many young people, her audience at the Royal Albert Hall hailed Meghan as ‘a global champion for the rights of women and girls, activist and philanthropist’.
Just two weeks later, the Sussexes departed for a remote waterfront mansion on Vancouver Island, the first stage of their dash to California. Harry would call that journey ‘running like hell’.
Since their exit from Britain in 2019, Meghan had devoted money

In Meghan’s new career, media advisors were supreme. At that moment, no one was more important in Los Angeles than Meghan’s in-house media managers: Beth Herlihy, Miranda Barbot and Ashley Hansen. Barbot had worked on Barack Obama’s 2012 election team and Hansen came from Universal Pictures. The most important outsider was Keleigh Thomas Morgan of Sunshine Sachs, a Los Angeles PR agency.

As the Sussexes’ global press secretary, Hansen described herself as ‘the brain behind Prince Harry and Meghan Markle’s PR and communications strategy’.Harry & Meghan and she was preparing for the launches of Prince Harry’s memoir Spare and Meghan’s Archetypes podcasts. Like Meghan, Hansen was a Californian, passionate about food, exercise and meditation.

Daily, Hansen and her team of four monitored every media outlet for coverage of their employer. ‘We know, especially with tabloids, there’s an agenda there,’ said Hansen. ‘And it’s a negative one for the most part.’ In anticipation of their arrival in Manchester, Herlihy admitted, ‘All bets are off with the British media. And the trolls are out in force.’ Snapping orders, Hansen was the salty voice of the Sussexes’ war machine.

Long before she arrived in Britain for the One Young World event in 2022, there was always a risk that the appearance would be used for Meghan’s own benefit rather than that of the charity. Building on her successful September issue for Vogue in 2019, Meghan asked the magazine’s editor Edward Enninful to feature her on Vogue’s front cover ahead of the Manchester event. Over the following weeks, their discussions soured. Meghan’s demands for control over the content reportedly infuriated Enninful. Plus, he had already chosen a cover Vogue’s forty ‘legendary’ women.

Hansen perfectly understood the challenge. Even the timing of Meghan’s appearance was a high-wire act. At the same time as she was due to walk on to the stage, the Conservative Party would announce that Liz Truss had been elected as the new Tory leader and the next prime minister. Remarkably, Hansen had negotiated that Sky TV would cut from Westminster to capture Meghan’s live appearance.

Hansen recognised that Meghan was sensitive to slights. To limit any embarrassment in Manchester, there was to be tight scrutiny of ticketing to exclude any protesters, a ban on spectators standing outside the venue, and all journalists were to be excluded from the hall. Hansen demanded that only Omid Scobie, a trusted author, and the photographer Misan Harriman were to be given access to the event. Harriman’s photos would be published only in Town & Country magazine, ordered Hansen.Vogue cover and photos of baby Archie, he’d had no contact from her ever since. In Meghan’s world, it seemed tradesmen were ignored until required.

Any protest that the media restrictions would damage Meghan’s request to be globally positioned as a philanthropist was dismissed by Keleigh Thomas Morgan. ‘KTM’s as useless as a chocolate teapot,’ scoffed an organiser. ‘Completely out of her depth.’ Yet Meghan knew no better. Risks were unacceptable to the Duchess.

‘I’ve been abandoned,’ complained Harry days before the event. Stressed about their security, Harry had been told that the Home Office refused to provide armed protection in Manchester and London. But with Meghan’s insistence that her appearance was essential to sustain

Before leaving Frogmore Cottage, still their Windsor home, Charlotte Tilbury herself had spent three hours on Meghan’s make-up. The final touch-up was completed at the Kimpton Clocktower Hotel in Manchester. After lunch and a rest, the couple were driven to The Bridgewater Hall. Over 2,300 eager young people were waiting. Herlihy refused the suggestion that they enter through the kitchen. They would brave the crowd. As they emerged on to the empty pavement, Harry heard ‘God Save the Queen’ booming from a circling van and a few distant boos. ‘They’re all paid by the tabloids to show up,’ Harry later pronounced.

Anxious about her speech, Meghan stood waiting off-stage while Harry, ever the professional, humoured his hosts with small talk. Hovering alongside was her publicist Ashley Hansen. Dressed in over-the-top Californian lifestyle clothes – a large trench coat, a big hat and sunglasses – Hansen symbolised Meghan’s similar discomfort of appearing in front of a British audience.

Escorted amid a standing ovation on to the stage, Meghan, wearing a red dress, adopted her rictus Hollywood smile, while Harry clapped with what was now arguably his perma-scowl. Meghan had abandoned the billed theme: ‘Ethical leadership – how can we instil transparency, honesty and integrity as core values for leaders?’ To Ella Robertson’s anger, her speech praised One Young World, who ‘saw in me what I wanted to see fully in myself’. And over the next ten minutes, she spoke exclusively about herself and her rise to fame.
‘She’s tone-deaf to others,’ Robertson realised. ‘She just can’t do what

Anxious about missing the train back to London, the Sussexes rushed from the building oblivious to the organisers’ indignation and the impression it gave that the couple felt that the British people were irrelevant and easy to ignore. In Meghan’s mind, the British and their country were irrelevant. Dissatisfied Brits were apparently easy to ignore, not least the Royal Family. She had accumulated all the ingredients for her career. She would appear to enjoy irritating her critics and enemies. Whatever had happened in the preceding months, Meghan clearly saw herself as the victor.

The next few days were hectic for the couple. Encouraged by Harry to play on the Royal Family’s popularity in Germany, the Invictus executives had arranged for a flight the next day on a Luftwaffe jet from RAF Northolt, a small airport in west London, to get the couple to their event in Düsseldorf. After landing, they were driven in a high-speed police convoy to the city’s town hall, where they were greeted at the end of a red carpet as heads of state by Mayor Stephan Keller. Unlike in Manchester, Harry was happy to be interviewed. ‘Great,’ he told the Invictus executives, clearly pleased to be feted.

On his return to London for the WellChild awards ceremony in the Lancaster Gate Hotel, Harry flourished as the popular prince – genuinely friendly towards the children and their parents and hailed as thoroughly good. At the end of the day, Harry returned to Frogmore anticipating his imminent return to Los Angeles. Deeply distrusted by the royal household, Harry had been purposely kept ignorant of events occurring in Scotland.
That night, 7th September 2022, Edward Young, the Queen’s private

Ever since Harry and Meghan met, on her initiative, on a blind date arranged in July 2016 while she was working at London’s Wimbledon tennis championships, Meghan had given thanks for her amazing luck. At the time, she was on the verge of losing her role in the cable TV series Suits and her Canadian boyfriend, Cory Vitiello, too. Landing Harry had transformed the life of a single, potentially unemployed 34-year-old actress who had just confessed to her publicist that she planned to freeze her eggs.

Scrambling through Google and newspaper profiles, she discovered how Harry was a confused character crying out for love and punished for his vulnerability. After their first meeting in London’s Soho House, she had called Harry to arrange a second meeting and then snapped up his invitation to stay with him at a safari camp in Botswana.

Over many days in the African bush, Meghan listened to his list of woes, clearly satisfying Harry’s quest for sympathy. She was so unlike his last long-term girlfriend, Cressida Bonas, the aspiring British actress who had tired of Harry’s conviction that spies were watching him through every window – even when they visited friends for dinner – and how he was a target to be shot. Bonas was exhausted from standing next to a

Unlike Bonas, who ended the relationship with Harry in 2014, Meghan appeared to be sympathetic to the torrent of his paranoid fears, much of it repeats of what he had told his therapist. ‘Generational trauma’ was the victims’ catchphrase to lament how their emotional needs were not satisfied. Meghan encouraged Harry’s certainty that anything he passionately believed must be true. Objective facts – namely, the real truth – were disposable if inconvenient. Within weeks, their mutual commitment was sealed. Harry was besotted by his new soulmate – a surrogate mother, homemaker, confessor, consigliera and safety net. Within months, cynical Palace officials were whispering about Meghan having ‘found a meal ticket’. Their suspicions outraged Harry.

Unreservedly, Harry identified with Meghan’s discomfort. ‘The hierarchy of my family,’ he said, was to blame. For thirty years, he complained, the institution had controlled and imprisoned him. Throughout his life, no one wanted to understand his frustration and his sense of inferiority. Few had listened to his need for an identity, any identity other than being the unloved ‘spare’. Beyond that personal torment, Harry was evidently too inarticulate and too adrift to be able to explain to Meghan the royal hierarchy. He felt himself pushed by his family to the side of the stage.

Aware of Harry’s complaints, the Palace had devoted enormous efforts during the months before their glittering wedding on 19th May 2018 – a glorious sunny day – to familiarise Meghan with the reality of life as a royal. Although Meghan would tell Oprah Winfrey in 2021 that Palace officials had never explained what she could expect, senior courtiers had obeyed the Queen’s orders to coach Meghan for her role in a constitutional monarchy.
Among the formalities designed to teach Meghan about her new life was the service to become a member of the Church of England.

To ensure that the 37-year-old American was aware of the complications of marrying into the Royal Family, Edward Lane Fox, Harry’s private secretary, presented a specially prepared thirty-point dossier to Meghan covering every aspect of royal life. It explained how, as patrons of hundreds of organisations, from lifeboats to the Royal Opera House and many community charities, the Royal Family embodied the heart of British society, applauding tens of thousands of generous volunteers. The politically powerless family represented the nation and its shared values, the glue that held Britain’s disparate society together. Some would conclude that Meghan did not read the dossier.

A parallel attempt was made by Samantha Cohen, appointed by the Queen to explain royal life and the monarch’s neutrality. The Queen’s genius was to disguise her beliefs by never revealing an opinion, especially about controversial causes. Dedicated to serving Britons and the Commonwealth, her primary duty was to cultivate goodwill, dissolve tension, and defend Christianity and the culture of her peoples. Palace officials suspected that all those conversations passed Meghan by. Harry would later write, ‘Meghan wasn’t given one minute of training.’

Already fearing disaster, a senior member of the Establishment was asked to explain personally to Meghan what to expect as Harry’s wife. Every month, he met Meghan in Kensington Palace for ninety minutes. Occasionally, Harry was present. Meghan, he discovered, dominated their relationship. Assuming the minor role, Harry appeared to be proudly besotted by his fiancée.
‘You’re not just marrying Harry Windsor,’ said the official, ‘you’re

After a few weeks, the senior official mentioned the Abdication – the decision in December 1936 by Edward VIII to renounce the throne in order to marry the American divorcee Wallis Simpson. After leaving Britain, Meghan was told, the Windsors had ended up in a cul-de-sac. She clearly sympathised with the Duchess of Windsor. But in her mind, she would dictate her own terms. Sycophantic submission was certainly not contemplated by the Californian.

The senior official grasped that Meghan saw herself at the centre of every situation. With a sense of her own important role, she anticipated remaining in total control of her own future. Any talk about hierarchy passed over her head. Lurking beneath the surface, he realised, was her refusal to be bound by Britain’s traditions. She would decide her actions for herself and if the Palace imposed constraints, then she would have the rules changed. If there were no changes, she would go. The official was startled. The marriage would be bumpy, he told an aide. If it lasted. He knew that no Palace official or member of the Royal Family would be able to control Meghan if she refused to conform. Everyone in Buckingham Palace would be powerless.

Another official lamented that the Californian refused to grasp that, ‘the Royal Family is the nation’s family and a national obsession. The royals may appear to be ordinary people but to maintain the monarchy, they relied on the public’s willingness to treat them like gods.’

Meghan’s unpredictability dawned on Palace officials when ‘family’ was mentioned. Meghan admitted that she brought baggage to the marriage. The model of a stable marriage had not existed in her own life. Her childhood, she said, had been difficult. Her relations with her family, she explained, were bad. Particularly with her father, Thomas Markle. Those speaking with her were surprised by her sense of isolation. But worse, she showed no interest in fostering better relations with Harry’s family. She refused to engage with those speaking about compromise and obedience. Fears about her intentions were confirmed after she disclosed, weeks before the ceremony, that ‘I hope my father won’t be coming to the wedding.’ The admission shocked her audience.

In the months before the wedding, officials noticed that Meghan had sidelined her father from the arrangements. He was not sent an invitation, nor did Meghan ask a British embassy official to offer him help, as she had in the case of her mother. More telling, Harry had not tried to meet Thomas Markle in Los Angeles. As the international media aggressively doorstepped the retired studio lighting technician’s home in Mexico, Meghan appeared to abandon him. Palace officials assumed Meghan had good reason to distance herself from Thomas Markle, especially after he famously lied about staging photographs of his preparations for the wedding. That was just one of many surprises causing alarm.

By then, those Palace officials closely involved had concluded that both Meghan and the Royal Family had ‘unrealistic expectations’ about her life after the marriage ceremony. For Meghan’s part, she clearly refused to accept any restrictions. On the other side, the Royal Family were deluding themselves. From the Queen down, they assumed she would somehow slot into her place. None of the senior royals understood

As Meghan set out her own expectations, a senior member of the Establishment noted Meghan’s rejection of royalty’s mystique. Uninterested in the monarchy’s place in Britain, she ignored the special aura that elevated the Royal Family. In her mind, the royals were more like super-celebrities.

Meghan spoke about The Firm, believing that the Queen ran the monarchy like the president of Universal Studios. She didn’t want to understand the labyrinthine network of personal relationships, interlinked organisations, livery companies and secret committees that comprised the Establishment. In her self-focused manner, she knew nothing about the ghosts of the past. Nor could she imagine the Queen’s private secretaries receiving calls from all over the world asking, ‘What does the Queen think?’ And, ‘What will she do?’

The Queen’s suspicions about Meghan surfaced before their wedding. To the monarch’s displeasure, Meghan insisted on making many of the decisions about the arrangements without consulting anyone. Not only did Meghan refuse to discuss with the Queen her wedding dress – the monarch complained it was ‘too white’ for a divorcee – but Meghan had approached the Archbishop of Canterbury to conduct the wedding without first consulting the Dean of Windsor.

Having made little effort to understand the reasons for global admiration of the Queen, Meghan, as the guest editor of Vogue in September 2019, would exclude the monarch from her list of fifteen ‘Game Changers’. The Queen’s senior advisors were not surprised. They acknowledged by then that the Queen’s efforts to welcome Meghan into the family had been rebuffed even before her wedding, notably by Meghan’s preference to sidestep protocols and traditions.

The report of Meghan’s 45-minute visit to the headquarters of the College of Arms located since 1555 in London’s Queen Victoria Street confirmed the Establishment’s worst fears. On the Queen’s orders, Meghan, the new Duchess of Sussex, was to have her own coat of arms. The design would be supervised by Thomas Woodcock, the Garter Principal King of Arms. Meghan’s arrival in Woodcock’s office was unexceptional. Every newly created peer visited to discuss his or her title and every aspect of Britain’s heraldry for their elevated position. The Californian had entered into an alien world of ancient British families, chivalry and their symbols. She would have been surprised to hear that Woodcook’s annual salary, set in 1618, was just £47, reduced from £100!

Woodcock’s task was to prevent any embarrassments but Meghan’s request was unprecedented. Convinced that she had been created a duchess in her own right, unrelated to Harry being the duke, she told Woodcock: ‘I want on the coat of arms a songbird with its tongue cut out.’ Woodcock was flummoxed. Any hint of nostalgia was quashed by her moral certitude. The Duchess eventually agreed to a nightingale, but without the image of it being forcibly silenced.
The Sound of Music. Her wedding was a feast of self-celebration.

After a somewhat unconventional ceremony, during which the TV cameras spotted Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall, stifling laughter and Kate, Duchess of Cambridge, wearing an unwaveringly stern expression, Meghan’s magnificent performance as she rode through Windsor in the open carriage with Harry was triumphant. Thanks to her Hollywood pedigree, she smiled majestically, waved gracefully and exuded genuine empathy to the crowds lining the streets. Many black Britons who identified with her had travelled especially to see the happy spectacle. By the end of the wedding day, which had cost £32 million, Meghan was confirmed as a global celebrity.

Those later dissecting the event with Kate were less enthusiastic. Meghan’s guest list for the ceremony and dinner included Hollywood celebrities whom she barely knew. The exclusion from the same dinner of some of Harry’s oldest friends did not go unnoticed. Meghan’s apparent dislike of rowdy Englishmen who shared Harry’s fondness for crude jokes had been detected. ‘She scoffed that on Fridays, Harry’s friends oiled their guns for the weekend,’ recalled a Palace official. That signalled,

Within six months, Meghan was irritated that there was little by the way of gushing praise for the few official duties she undertook. Compared to Hollywood, everything in Kensington Palace was strait-laced and straightforward. She felt unrecognised and unrewarded. Meghan’s unwillingness to accept the Royal Family’s diffidence towards Hollywood celebrity was matched by the Royal Family’s reluctance to bestow outright approval of her. The excited fizz among officials after her arrival had waned as her bolshiness grew. And no one became more aggrieved about Meghan’s subversion of royal tradition than William and Kate.
‘If you don’t mind, get your finger out of my face,’ Meghan Markle snapped at William. The teatime reconciliation meeting between the brothers and their wives had spun out of control.

Tea had been served in the usual traditional atmosphere befitting a medieval palace. The formalities failed to camouflage the tension. Sitting in the Cambridges’ recently redecorated twenty-room Kensington Palace apartment, soon after the Sussexes returned from their honeymoon in June 2018, Harry watched the confrontation escalate. William’s dislike of Meghan had been obvious after his warning to Harry before their engagement. ‘It’s gone too quickly,’ William had said about the speed of Harry’s relationship with ‘the American actress’. William’s suspicion of Meghan was echoed by his wife.

Kate, Harry was convinced, saw Meghan as a threat. The antagonism had started once their engagement was announced. Kate had not appreciated a streetwise, self-confident rival. Clever and stylish, Meghan was clearly unwilling to obediently serve the British people as a member of the royal team. As Meghan would later tell the tennis champion Serena Williams, the Royal Family ‘hates’ ambition. That was associated with vulgar pushiness.

As they entered the Cambridges’ apartment, Harry had spotted Kate’s disapproval of Meghan’s torn jeans. Previously, Kate had disapproved of Meghan wearing jeans at Wimbledon. Meghan’s behaviour, Kate decided, was incomprehensible. After all, Meghan would not have gone to the Kentucky Derby in ripped denim.

Harry had been similarly distraught about Kate’s visible ‘disgust’ after Meghan asked to share her lip gloss. Watching Meghan scrape gloss on to her finger, Kate’s glacial freeze had destroyed any suggestion of friendship.‘I was a hugger,’ recalled Meghan. ‘I didn’t realise that that is really jarring for a lot of Brits.’ In Harry’s opinion, while Meghan ‘spread kindness’, the Cambridges responded with spite.

Naturally, Meghan’s undisguised disdain for the Palace’s hierarchy had aroused Kate’s distrust. Kate did not hide her own suspicions. Her restraint was seen as hostility. For her part, Meghan was contemptuous of the Cambridges’ aloofness, it being a far cry from her own personal style: Kate was boring; William had cried himself to sleep at Eton. Both were so detached from her reality.

Meghan believed that Harry was right to feel the Cambridges were jealous of her successful career – just like the Royal Family had been envious of Diana’s. Even after two years in London, Meghan did not understand how seriously the Cambridges and all members of the Royal Family took their roles. The Royal Family was a job. Duty, hard work and self-sacrifice without a big pay cheque was alien to someone brought up in Hollywood. But Meghan had laughed off Charles’s

In this clash of two worlds, Meghan was irritated by the royals’ inflexible hierarchy. More than ever, she resented their ordered and impersonal lifestyle, and that birth dictated seniority. Impatient with the protocol of the royals’ relationships, she mocked that even in private encounters, relations curtsied to acknowledge another’s seniority. She could not understand that the curtsy was to preserve the royals’ aura. How ridiculous, she thought and proclaimed so publicly in years to come, that Harry greeted the Queen with a bow of his head followed by ‘Your Majesty. Granny!’

With that certainty, there was no mystery about Meghan’s unwillingness to curtsy to Kate. Her own talent and popularity, in that reckoning, proved her superiority. Meghan gave the impression that she regarded Kate as an untalented prude, who ought to show the respect she, Meghan, deserved. As seeming proof, Kate had recently hired Virginia Chadwyck-Healey, a former Vogue editor and stylist, to match Meghan with a youthful, lighter haircut and a new wardrobe.

While Meghan’s certainty of purpose had seduced Harry, her talents had alarmed his brother and Kate. Both knew Harry’s problems – he’d had a damaged youth, confused by drink, drugs and therapy after his mother’s death, and had been unable to cement a balanced relationship with a woman. Both evidently saw Meghan as a threat rather than an ally. Yet Meghan didn’t seem to understand that she could not compete with Kate, the future Queen, nor accept that her task was to support her.

The Cambridges believed that Meghan was not reconciled to what was deemed her destiny in London. She lamented Harry’s unlikely chances of one day being King, just as she regretted the possibility that

So, on that fateful day, amid all those conflicting, undisguised emotions, the two couples engaged in strained small talk over tea. Then the conversation switched to the cause of their disagreements. In July 2019, Meghan rejected the Queen’s invitation to visit Balmoral. Archie, she explained, was too young to fly. Days later, though, she flew with her two-month-old baby to the South of France as guests of Elton John. The world assumed that the Sussexes’ jaunt on Elton John’s private jet was for a holiday. No one knew that the singer had arranged for Harry to meet David Sherborne, a barrister specialising in suing newspapers.

Aged fifty, Sherborne was mocked as a vain, slick operator dressed in unusually tight suits with dyed hair and skin toned by a sunbed. He was also notorious for his colourful relationships with women. Following two marriages and two divorces, he had brushed with scandal after enjoying an affair with a fellow lawyer while both worked on the Leveson inquiry, a controversial investigation in 2011 into the illegal

Sherborne was on a mission. Consumed by his desire to expose newspapers’ crimes, he had represented actors and entertainment professionals, including Elton John and Hugh Grant. His loathing of newspapers appeared to be matched by his desire to push himself in front of cameras alongside his famous clients. Adding Harry would naturally hugely boost his campaign. Not only would a royal’s inclusion be unprecedented but the chances of success would improve. ‘We needed someone with money,’ Hugh Grant admitted in an ITV documentary.

In the warm, affluent atmosphere of Elton John’s Mediterranean villa, Harry willingly fell into Sherborne’s lap. His sales patter was no doubt enticing. Among his past clients, said Sherborne, were Michael Douglas, Tony Blair and Harry’s mother, Princess Diana. Critically, as a lawyer in the Leveson inquiry, Sherborne had seen the list of payments by the newspapers to private investigators between 2005 and 2011. Only with great reluctance had the newspapers handed over that potentially incriminating evidence, relying on Judge Leveson’s promise that their documents would never be published. They would be sealed by law, promised Leveson, and could not be published without Ministerial permission. Sherborne did not forget what he had read.

Sherborne added a critical ingredient to his fluent sales patter. In 2015, he had won a notable victory against the Mirror Group.The judge, Anthony Mann, had decided that the Mirror’s journalists and editors were immersed in a widespread culture of ‘extensive and habitual’ unlawful activities including hacking telephone voicemails.
Eight claimants won against the Mirror. Rival lawyers were stunned by

To Harry’s glee, Sherborne added that Rupert Murdoch’s News UK was equally culpable. Rebekah Brooks, the former editor of the News of the World and the Sun, had been identified by Leveson as knowing about her journalists’ unlawful activities. Brooks had been prosecuted and acquitted, Sherborne acknowledged, but she could still be sued to recover damages. News UK’s records, continued Sherborne, showed hundreds of payments by their newspapers to private investigators. Among their targets was Harry’s former girlfriend, the Zimbabwean Chelsy Davy. Unlawfully, they had hacked into her voicemail, emails and Facebook account, it would later be claimed.

Sherborne then surely delivered the final inducement: the litigation would cost Harry very little. He would bear no financial risk. The reason was that all the claimants’ lawyers worked on a contingency basis – no win, no fee. All Harry needed was an insurance policy to cover the newspapers’ costs in the unlikely event he lost. And Sherborne had never lost. That formula would earn Sherborne at least £40 million and enrich Temple Legal Protection, the insurance company which pocketed a large slice of the damages awarded by the judges to the claimants. To monopolise the litigation, Sherborne and the solicitors staggered their cases: first the Mirror, next News UK, and finally they would target the Daily Mail group.
Sherborne, Harry decided, was ‘a lovely fellow who knew more about the hacking scandal than anyone I’d ever met’.
For over ten years, Harry believed, the tabloids had targeted him by unlawful means. Sherborne crafted his sentiments about the tabloids:

Trusting the lawyer, Harry admitted: ‘I could have been hacked on a daily basis. I simply don’t know.’ All he knew was their intrusion had been horrendous. Similarly, he also said that Meghan was the ‘prey’ for online ‘hunters’.

Harry went further though. He blamed the media for his own mistakes – drunkenness, drugs, wearing a Nazi uniform at a party and being filmed nude at a Las Vegas hotel. Newspapers, he believed, should have suppressed the truth. He made no allowance that other royals had suffered worse. Not least Charles and Camilla, with the excruciatingly embarrassing ‘Tampongate’ recording in 1989, and his mother with ‘Squidgygate’ – taped calls of her conversations in the late 1980s with her lover James Gilbey.
How, Harry asked Sherborne, could he ‘find a way to stop the abuse, intrusion and hate that was coming towards me and my wife?’
‘Why not hire your own lawyer?’ asked Sherborne. By suing newspapers he could discover if he had been hacked. That had never occurred to Harry.

On their return to London, Harry consulted the Royal Family’s trusted solicitor Gerrard Tyrrell of Harbottle & Lewis. Yet the lawyer advised against suing News Group. Swiftly, Meghan convinced Harry that Tyrrell was ‘taking directions from Clarence House’. The same solicitor had advised Meghan not to sue the Mail on Sunday for breaching her privacy and copyright by publishing excerpts from her letter to her father. Meghan had ignored Tyrrell and the Palace and, on Sherborne’s advice, hired Schillings.
Keith Schillings is an aggressive London solicitor not previously employed by the Royal Family. Proudly, he is the antithesis of Tyrrell, a pukka West End lawyer.

Before he acted, Harry called William. His brother suggested that ‘I seek permission from Granny.’ Harry called the Queen and, he claimed, she agreed he could bring an action. Palace officials were not consulted. The timetable for what followed was set by Schillings.

Relying on Sherborne and his publicists, Harry also agreed to issue court claims against Mirror Group Newspapers and News UK for their unlawful activities. At the same time, Meghan would ignore the Palace’s advice and claim against the Mail on Sunday. Schillings’ argument was precise: Meghan’s relationship with her father was private. No public interest could justify breaching her copyright and privacy by publishing her letter.
Attacking the media would be the Sussexes’ curtain-raiser to leaving Britain. Their declaration of war was to be broadcast in South Africa.

On the eve of leaving for a ten-day official tour of South Africa on 23rd September, Beth Herlihy, a private secretary in Kensington Palace, noticed that Meghan was suffering from ‘baby blues’. ‘She’s having a breakdown,’ Herlihy confided. ‘She’s very unhappy and Harry can’t fix it.’

Over the previous months, Palace officials had devoted huge effort to please the Sussexes’ constantly changing demands. Their procrastination and indecisiveness had, in the opinion of one senior Palace advisor, become a ‘sickness’. Unexpectedly, even Archie became a tool of the Sussexes’ brand. Meghan insisted that their son, at only four months old, should also come on the official tour to South Africa. Palace officials were puzzled. Just three weeks earlier, Meghan had refused to visit the Queen in Balmoral because Archie was too young to fly. Some now understood her intentions and, more significantly, Harry’s insincerity.
During the first days in South Africa, the tour was hugely successful. Corralled British journalists, kept away from the Sussexes, ecstatically

Unreported was the Sussexes’ conduct. Both Meghan and Harry were ignoring advice from all their courtiers. Sara Latham, on the Sussexes’ media staff, was seen in tears and another member of her staff condemned Meghan as ‘a nightmare’. Jason Knauf and the human resources director Samantha Carruthers had both resigned. Carruthers complained of being ‘treated harshly’. Those convulsions remained unreported by the media, but worse was imminent. Harry planned to deliver a bombshell statement prepared by his lawyers in London.

Ignoring pleas by Palace officials not to ruin the tour, Harry fired his first shot. Journalists in South Africa were directed to read Harry’s post on the Sussexes’ official website that had been drafted by Sherborne. The Prince denounced the ‘press pack’ for ‘relentless propaganda’ against Meghan by pursuing ‘a game, and one that we have been unwilling to play from the start’. Harry continued, ‘Unfortunately, my wife has become one of the latest victims of a British tabloid press that wages campaigns against individuals with no thought to the consequences, a ruthless campaign that has escalated over the past year, throughout her pregnancy and while raising our newborn son. There is a human cost to this relentless propaganda, specifically when it is knowingly false and malicious, and though we have continued to put on a brave face, as so many of you can relate to, I cannot begin to describe how painful it has been. Because in today’s digital age, press fabrications are repurposed as truth across the globe. One day’s coverage is no longer tomorrow’s chip paper.’

Clearly forgetting her New York baby shower, Harry claimed that the media had created ‘lie after lie at her expense simply because she has not been visible while on maternity leave’. While failing to identify

Finally, he referenced Princess Diana, the omnipresent shadow of his mother. Meghan, he said, was ‘falling victim to the same powerful forces’ and his ‘deepest fear is history repeating itself. I’ve seen what happens when someone I love is commoditised to the point that they are no longer treated or seen as a real person. I lost my mother and now I watch my wife falling victim to the same powerful forces.’

Shortly afterwards, legal firm Schillings issued a High Court claim on Meghan’s behalf against the Mail on Sunday. British judges, Keith Schilling knew, could be trusted to protect the Royal Family from embarrassment. The Mail on Sunday, Meghan claimed, was ‘deliberately stirring up’ a dispute between Meghan and Thomas Markle as part of an ‘agenda’. Her father, described as a ‘vulnerable and fragile individual’ had been ‘exploited . . . harassed and manipulated’ by the media. Hours later, Thomas Markle robustly ridiculed those suggestions.

‘Hysterical’, ‘ill-advised’, ‘hot-headed’, ‘self-indulgent’ and ‘over-emotional’ were a few of the newspapers’ verdicts about Harry’s statement and Meghan’s High Court claim. The mood on the tour turned sour. The Sussexes had tainted months of hard work to improve Britain’s status in South Africa.

The third assault, a full-frontal attack on the Royal Family, was simultaneously being filmed in South Africa for ITV by Tom Bradby, a trusted friend of Harry’s. The TV documentary, Harry & Meghan: An African Journey, was meant to focus on the Sussexes talking about South Africa’s human tragedies. Instead, the Sussexes used Bradby to issue their declaration of war.

Meghan provided an even more explosive interview. Clearly rehearsed, she dramatically bit her lip in the falling light as she whispered to Bradby that she felt ‘vulnerable’ during her pregnancy, how the pressure was ‘hard’ and the experience of ‘just trying to be a new mother or trying to be a newlywed’ was ‘made really challenging’. As tears welled up, she blamed the British tabloids for trying to destroy her life. American newspapers, she emphasised, would never criticise her. ‘I really tried to adopt this sensibility of the British stiff upper lip . . . I never thought this would be easy, but I thought it would be fair.’ Though many will sympathise with the emotional plight of a new mother, it should not be forgotten that Meghan had boasted she could produce tears on demand.

Would it be fair, asked Bradby, to say that she is ‘not really OK’? Another tear dramatically fell as Meghan replied with a swipe at the Royal Family: ‘Yes. And also, thank you for asking, because not many people have asked if I’m OK. But it’s a very real thing to be going through behind the scenes.’ With a hint of more tears, she deftly added another rehearsed line: ‘It’s not enough to just survive something, right? That is not the point of life. You’ve got to thrive.’

Harry was summoned to Buckingham Palace to meet Edward Young and Clive Alderton, the Queen’s and Charles’s private secretaries. Tall with floppy hair, Alderton, aged fifty-two, educated at Abingdon School, had entered the Foreign Office despite not going to university. After serving as a diplomat, he had been appointed to work in Clarence House in 2006, shortly after Charles’s and Camilla’s marriage. To have survived in Charles’s service for that long proved Alderton had exceptional talent never to challenge his employer. As a charming old-school courtier, he could both reassure Charles of his obedience but also steer his employer in the right direction.

By contrast, Young, aged fifty-three, bespectacled and stubby, was a cautious former corporate head of communications who had been deputy to Christopher Geidt, the Queen’s legendary and loyal private secretary. Geidt had been ousted in 2017 by Charles and Prince Andrew for seeking to control their dangerous excesses. Both princes were involved in what Geidt believed were damaging financial activities. And in Andrew’s case, particularly his relationship with the American paedophile Jeffrey Epstein. Geidt wanted to control both princes from Buckingham Palace. Yet despite his impressive military bearing, Geidt’s weakness was arguably his lack of leadership qualities. After his dismissal, the royal households were staffed by contrary courtiers – especially Young versus Alderton – each guarding their fiefdoms,
In the case of Harry’s war against the media, Alderton and Young were united. Both ‘demanded’ that Harry did not sue News UK.

ITV planned to broadcast Bradby’s South African interviews during the Cambridges’ official visit to Pakistan. The tour was important both for British diplomacy and to showcase Kate’s transformation during her maternity leave into a more glamorous, self-confident woman. Harry was asked to negotiate with ITV to delay the broadcast. He refused. It was a move that was judged to be wanton sabotage of the Cambridges. An almost unnoticed casualty was another TV documentary, crafted to promote Camilla.

On 12th November, Hillary Clinton visited Meghan at Frogmore House. As Meghan unloaded her anxieties, Clinton offered her unqualified understanding. Meghan’s treatment, Clinton believed, was ‘heart-breaking and wrong’. In the politician’s opinion, Meghan’s meteoric rise was being crushed by the Royal Family. The Sussexes, she believed, were ‘struggling to have a life of meaning and integrity on their own terms’.

By then, Meghan’s publicist Keleigh Thomas Morgan had found a temporary home for the Sussexes – a secluded seaside mansion on Vancouver Island, conveniently north of Los Angeles. Three weeks later, the Sussexes arrived at Mille Fleurs, a luxurious five-bedroomed waterfront mansion where they would be guarded by six Canadian government protection officers. Their stay had been arranged by the 71-year-old Canadian music producer David Foster, a friend and

Officially, the Sussexes would not spend Christmas at Sandringham after all. Their spokesman briefed that the couple would establish a presence in a Commonwealth country. From Vancouver, said her spokeswoman, Meghan could ‘resume her business activities’.
Harry and Meghan knew exactly what they wanted from the Royal Family – status, money and protection. Only eighteen months after their wedding, they were negotiating their exit.

Under Meghan’s guidance, Harry had composed an email to his father setting out their demands. Fearing it would be leaked to the media over Christmas, he held it back. He was unaware that a member of his own staff had passed it to Dan Wootton, the Sun’s showbiz editor. But, concerned about a backlash after the Palace had strongly denied the truth about the Sussexes leaving Britain, the newspaper’s editor refused to publish Wootton’s scoop.The Sussexes, wrote Wootton, planned to divide their time in North America between representing the Crown and, at the same time, using their royal titles to earn money.

Even without reading Harry’s email, Charles and the Queen knew from telephone conversations with him that he wanted to disengage from royal service in Britain. Not least because among his proposals, Harry suggested that if a solution could not be found, ‘we would be willing to relinquish our Sussexes titles, if need be’.

Unsure how to resolve the Sussexes’ demands, Young consulted Charles’s private secretary, Clive Alderton. Alderton’s suspicions about Meghan were shared by Simon Case, William’s secretary. By then, the three had discussed Knauf’s complaints about Meghan’s mistreatment of her staff but had done absolutely nothing. Meghan’s staff remained unprotected and she had not been formally advised to control her behaviour. All three had obeyed their bosses: the Queen, Charles and William. Nothing, the three had decided, should be done to further inflame the Sussexes.

All three private secretaries had similar qualities. They were solid, loyal and decent. They were also cautious courtiers accustomed not to take the initiative. Their survival depended on giving advice that they knew would please their royal masters. Stepping out of line was unimaginable. Notoriously, Charles instantly dismissed anyone who failed to give him unquestioned obedience, whereas the Queen usually listened to advice before making a decision. William was known to take advice but occasionally failed to control his temper. All three private secretaries understood that the royal command was final. In those circumstances they had failed during November to fully persuade the Royal Family that the Sussex crisis, brewing since the wedding eighteen months earlier, was about to escalate.

Confirmation of the Sussexes’ real intentions arrived in London just before Christmas 2019 from the British High Commission in Ottawa and the Canadian government. Conversations with the Sussexes and others indicated that the couple intended to stay in North America and never return permanently to Britain. The Queen was not surprised by Young’s report that the Sussexes weren’t coming back: ‘Of course not. They took the dogs.’

Discussions were to begin again in the new year. Before leaving Vancouver for London on 5th January with Meghan, Harry expected to head from Heathrow to Sandringham for tea with the Queen and to stay overnight. During those hours, he planned to persuade his grand-mother to approve their planned semi-exile. Later, Harry would claim that the Queen was not surprised that he went to America. ‘She knew what was going on. She knew how hard it was. She never said to me she was angry. I think she was sad it got to that point.’

But other opinions would suggest that Harry had deluded himself. The Queen’s closest officials recorded that the Queen was mystified by the Sussexes’ behaviour. She did not trust Meghan and suspected Harry had been manipulated. She was easily persuaded by Charles and William that the Sussexes should not be invited to Sandringham after they arrived. Accordingly, just before boarding the plane, Harry was told that the arrangement was cancelled. He called his grandmother. ‘There’s something in my diary I didn’t know I had,’ the Queen told him. ‘I didn’t know I was busy. I’ve been told that I’m busy all week.’

During the nine-hour flight, Harry fumed. ‘They’re blocking a grandson from seeing his grandmother,’ he told Meghan. He blamed his family’s three private secretaries. ‘I’d spent my life dealing with courtiers, scores of them,’ Harry would recall. ‘But now I dealt with just three, all middle-aged white men who’d managed to consolidate power through a series of bold Machiavellian manoeuvres.’

The following year, with the help of the ghostwriter of his autobiography, Harry would categorise the three private secretaries. Simon Case, William’s private secretary, known in Kensington Palace as ‘master of circumvention’, was called the Fly: ‘The Fly had spent much of his career adjacent to and indeed drawn to shit. The offal of government

Clive Alderton, Charles’s private secretary, was the Wasp: ‘He was great at pretending to be polite, even servile . . . A short while later, without warning, he’d give you such a stab with his outsized stinger that you’d cry out in confusion: Where the fuck did that come from?’
Edward Young was dubbed the Bee: ‘Poised that people didn’t fear him [which was] a big mistake, sometimes their last mistake.’

Harry’s colourful exaggeration of the men’s abilities was dictated by his attitude towards those who refused to give him what he wanted: ‘I disliked these men and they didn’t have any use for me. They considered me irrelevant at best, stupid at worst.’ In his naivety Harry refused to understand that the private secretaries owed their loyalty to the interest of the monarchy and the Queen, and to no one else. Their task was to protect the Crown.

After landing in London, Harry telephoned Young. Controlling his emotions, Harry described his mental health problems. Unable to cope with the stress, he told Young it would be best to make a public statement and return immediately to Canada. Young urged Harry not to annoy the Queen. She expected to see him at Sandringham after the various options had been considered. That would happen within one week.

Yet the following morning, 7th January, the Sun published Harry’s emailed proposals of giving up a life of public service. Harry was incandescent. Charles, he was sure, had leaked his email to sabotage the negotiations. Nothing, he raged to Meghan, had changed in six years. It was just the Palace’s latest smear of ‘leaking and planting of stories against me and my wife’.

Prompted by the leak, Harry emailed his proposals to Charles on 8th January 2020. At the same time, he and Meghan issued a public statement that they would be ‘stepping back’ as full-time senior royals. In future, they would split their time between Britain and North America

On the same day, the three private secretaries met in Alderton’s office in Clarence House with an official from the Cabinet Office to discuss the Sussexes’ fate. They planned to wait for Meghan to return to her son in Canada before inviting Harry to Sandringham. The three knew that the Queen had some sympathy for Charles’s second son. Her own father had faced the same predicament, as had her sister Margaret. The family had never fully considered the needs of the ‘spare’. She wanted to accommodate the Sussexes, as long as they didn’t try to have it both ways. But she was not prepared to allow them – especially Meghan – to earn money while retaining their royal status. Exploiting the Crown for commercial enrichment was unacceptable. There could be no compromise, the Queen insisted. The three private secretaries understood that. Meghan did not. Harry was too consumed by rage to grasp one truth: what many saw as Meghan’s betrayal of the Royal Family was matched by his own wish to destabilise it.

Harry understood that Meghan planned to exploit the Royal Family label. The foundations of Meghan’s monetisation of the monarchy had been already laid by her Los Angeles business advisors. They had registered in Delaware a slew of domestic items – pens to clothing – to be sold under the Sussex Royal logo. In their statement, the Sussexes said they planned to ‘work to become financially independent’.

On 11th January, Edward Young sent Harry a draft proposal, written by Simon Case, with the five options for the arrangements for his future life. The options ranged from carrying on as working royals to the full severance as representatives of the Royal Family with the loss of security. This, option five, was not acceptable to the Sussexes. Meghan encouraged Harry’s conviction that he could deliver her wishes during the summit negotiations in Sandringham. With that, she returned to Vancouver but intended to join the summit over Zoom on her return.

Some recall Harry’s meeting in Sandringham with Charles, William, the Queen and their private secretaries as tense but sober. By contrast, Harry, commenting on the last time he would sit around a table with his family, regarded them all as ‘cold’ and William as a ‘rolling catastrophe’. Fearful of Meghan’s popularity, Harry would say that his family were terrified of the Sussexes. ‘It was terrible,’ Harry said, ‘to have my brother scream and shout at me, and my father say things that simply weren’t true and my grandmother quietly sit there and sort of take it all in.’

Harry’s portrayal of the Queen as a sweet but powerless puppet operating at the mercy of darker, superior forces, obeying orders from courtiers and her family, exposed his misunderstanding of reality. Harry couldn’t imagine that the Queen was horrified by his behaviour. She hated confrontation and was aware that marriage had changed him for the worse. Although his grandmother undoubtedly cherished their close relationship, she cared more about her family as a whole – and the monarchy. Harry, she realised, was beyond reason. He would only listen to Meghan. ‘You’re either in or you’re out,’ the Queen declared.

At the end of ninety minutes, Young distributed a draft of the statement the Palace would be publishing. Option five was the outcome. Ordering Harry to give up all his appointments and his HRH title, the Prince was told that neither he nor Meghan could use their royal titles to earn money.

The public statement, approved by the Queen, did leave the door open, though. While the Sussexes would ‘always be much-loved members of my family’ the Queen publicly acknowledged, ‘I recognise the challenges they have experienced as a result of intense scrutiny over the last two years and support their wish for a more independent life.’ She hoped ‘they would start building a happy and peaceful life’. In return, Harry pledged that the Sussexes would always remain loyal servants but he left his family unable to understand why the Queen had ruled out the compromise he wanted.

For Harry, a defining moment came as he was driven from Sandringham through the flat Norfolk countryside back to London. Kensington Palace, Harry heard on the radio, had responded to a newspaper report that Harry and Meghan were leaving Britain because William had bullied the Sussexes. Kensington Palace’s rebuttal described the newspaper’s accusation as ‘false and offensive’. To Harry’s fury, the statement was issued in both his and William’s names. ‘I couldn’t believe it,’ Harry later said. ‘No one had asked my permission to put my name to a statement like that.’ He telephoned Meghan. ‘She burst into floods of tears because they were happy to lie to protect my brother, yet for three years they were never willing to tell the truth to protect us.’
Harry blamed William for assuming the responsibility of protecting

Meghan believed that it was a critical moment: ‘Suddenly what clicked in my head was “It’s never going to stop”.’ Harry agreed, explaining later: ‘So there was no other option at this point. I said, “We need to get out of here.”’

The naivety of Edward Young and the other courtiers emerged on the eve of Harry’s return to Canada. At the Palace’s request, Boris Johnson, the prime minister, was asked to persuade Harry to reconsider his proposed exile. Meeting Harry in east London, Johnson realised before the end of their twenty-minute conversation that he was immersed in ‘a ridiculous business . . . when they made me try to persuade Harry to stay. Kind of manly pep talk. Totally hopeless.’

Back on the remote Vancouver Island, Harry and Meghan grappled with the reality of isolation. His family, Harry concluded, had decided to make the Sussexes irrelevant. The summit was ‘just for show’. Harry could not accept that Young had simply obeyed the Queen’s orders.

The public’s reaction to ‘Megxit’ was divided. Some were outraged by the Sussexes’ ingratitude and self-destruction after being universally welcomed on their wedding day. A substantial minority were convinced that the Sussexes were the victims of the merciless white Royal Family. ‘Black Britons know why Meghan wants out,’ wrote Afua Hirsch in the New York Times. ‘It’s the racism.’

Heathrow’s Windsor Lounge is a sanctuary for the rich and famous. For about £3,000, an airline passenger can buy privacy and supreme comfort before being driven personally to the aircraft’s steps. Undoubtedly, the building has been a watershed for many lives and none more than Prince Harry’s as he waited for his return flight to Vancouver in January 2020.

In the hours since the battle in Sandringham, Meghan had hastened to confirm their commercial agreements. Her business manager and agent in Los Angeles assured her that a contract with Netflix was certain. Filming could start as soon as they arrived in California. Oprah Winfrey was eagerly waiting. Meghan also encouraged Harry to see that monetising his anger was the best cure. Everything he said would change their lives for the better because he was right. Betrayal, she knew, was a good story, and evidently best told on TV and as an autobiography.

Meghan arranged for Jennifer Rudolph Walsh at William Morris Endeavor to be confirmed as Harry’s literary agent. Promoting herself as one of America’s most successful agents with links to Oprah Winfrey and other women celebrities, the steely Walsh acknowledged Harry’s promise to deliver a brutal exposé of his life within the Royal Family. The Prince never considered his hypocrisy.
‘It’s amazing what people will do when offered a huge amount of

Similarly, while Meghan complained that her father gave TV and magazine interviews despite being told not to speak, she too was about to give interviews to Vogue, People magazine, Oprah Winfrey and Netflix.

All Meghan’s planning since her marriage was bearing fruit. She was well advanced in the process of recruiting a director for Archewell, her Californian commercial organisation dedicated to ‘illuminating, thought-provoking and diverse narratives that underscore our common humanity and celebrate community’. Their later protestations of arriving penniless in America were inaccurate.

Yet secluded on Vancouver Island, Harry’s indecisiveness delayed the couple’s next step.Harry feared making the final break to Los Angeles. All his promises at Sandringham about working loyally for the Crown while living in Canada and not financially exploiting his royal status would be shown as worthless.

But first they would return to London on 5th March to stage a Hollywood-style farewell. Astutely, Meghan choreographed photo opportunities in the City, while Harry made an emotional farewell to the Royal Marines at the Royal Albert Hall. Jointly, they made a final

To ensure that her message was publicised to her fans, she told a ‘friend’ – a publicist – to deliver her parting words to the Daily Mail: ‘I gave up my entire life for this family. I was willing to do whatever it takes, but here we are. It’s very sad.’ In words that sound accompanied by violins, her curated elegy appealed to her supporters, including ITV’s Tom Bradby. Obligingly, the journalist went on to criticise the ‘racist’ Royal Family: ‘Few meaningful attempts were made by anyone to heal the wounds.’New York Times endorsed her contrived lament. The Sussexes, the newspaper pronounced, had left London for ‘an uncertain life as semi-royals’ in ‘a distant frontier outpost’.

Soon after their return to Vancouver Island – just as his Palace staff had forewarned Harry – the Canadian government announced that the 24-hour taxpayer-funded police protection around their remote home would be withdrawn. ‘Vipers,’ Harry complained, publicising his fear about not being ‘able to protect my son’.
In March, Jennifer Rudolph Walsh advised Harry that Penguin Random House in New York was likely to make the best bid for his autobiography. If three bestselling books were delivered, the Sussexes

Always fearful of lacking sufficient money but also aware that her celebrity should now be more fruitful than the kind of freebies she had extracted from her lifestyle blog The Tig, Meghan sought the loan of a suitable home in Los Angeles and a free private jet to take all their possessions from Vancouver. She relied on Keleigh Thomas Morgan. The publicist didn’t understand Britain or the Royal Family, but specialised in Hollywood celebrity. The two agreed that Meghan should appeal for help as the target of the Royal Family’s racism. Their pleas were answered by Tyler Perry, a high-profile black film producer. On 14th March, shortly before American air space was restricted by the Covid crisis, the Sussexes loaded all their possessions on to Perry’s private jet and flew to Los Angeles. The countdown to pocketing millions of dollars from their royal lives began on that flight.

‘Good morning,’ said Harry after pressing ‘video’ on his mobile phone. ‘It’s 6am on 14th March and we are on the Freedom Flight. We are leaving Canada and we are headed to Los Angeles.’ He added a lament about escaping the ‘level of hate that is being stirred up in the last three years, particularly against my wife and my son’.

The melodramatic statement had been suggested by their Los Angeles agent. Netflix, he said, would pay more if they delivered hours of private videos shot in their most intimate moments. Meghan understood perfectly. She had been collecting that material since she met Harry, not least when he proposed to her.
For Harry, it was different. He was escaping what he later called ‘the constant harassment (online and off), intimidation and abuse that my wife and I have suffered at the hands of the tabloids’.
By the end of the day, the Sussexes were settling into Tyler Perry’s eight-bedroom home in Beverly Ridge set in twenty-two acres. ‘For many reasons,’ said Meghan, ‘I’m glad to be home.’

Meghan’s return was welcomed by her supporters – a coalition of feminists, Democrats, liberals and anti-monarchists. Peggy Drexler, a research psychologist sympathetic to Meghan, typified those who accepted Meghan’s claim that what she demanded most was total privacy: ‘They no longer wished to be trailed by the media, obsessed over, watched. Harry didn’t want to be constantly reminded of his mother’s tragic death every time he and Meghan were photographed; Meghan didn’t want to be tabloid fodder for what she wore, how she did her hair, where she was from or how much she did or didn’t enjoy the spotlight. They wanted to reach financial independence, live as regular people, raise their children in private.’

Placed in the third row and glaring at William’s back, Harry sat beside Jack Brooksbank, Princess Eugenie’s husband. ‘I’m fed up with the way they treat me,’ Harry told the marketing executive who had left university without a degree to become a bartender. ‘It’s not an ideal situation. They don’t care.’

After the Crown of St Edward had been safely placed on Charles’s head, Harry intoned, ‘God Save King Charles. Long Live King Charles. May the King live forever.’ The camera did not focus on Harry as his father’s former mistress was crowned Queen. The heralds shouted Vivat Regina Camilla’. In other circumstances, they would have loudly proclaimed ‘Vivat Regina Diana’.

Harry could have reflected that the crowning of Camilla, an unpopular divorcee, as Queen had proved Charles’s tenacity. In a previous era that would have been impossible. But Harry’s family were romantics. Just as Harry had sacrificed everything for Meghan, Edward VIII had abdicated the throne for Wallis Simpson. The passing camera shot of Harry caught the face of a sad man.

To Harry’s visible irritation, after the historic symbols were passed around Charles, William stepped forward to collaborate in the most solemn rituals. Paying homage, William knelt in front of his father, pledged his loyalty – ‘So help me God’ – and then kissed his father on the cheek. Harry saw an unusual bond – an intense relationship – the same one he had sacrificed and now sought to destroy. At that moment, the future of the monarchy appeared to be secure for three generations. The caravan had moved on without him.

King Charles rose from his throne and headed alone towards the abbey’s exit. After years of controversy, he had fulfilled his destiny. More consciously than others in his family, Charles recognised how other European monarchies had been swept aside because of extravagance, stubbornness or scandal. The greatest threat to the British monarchy, the new King feared, was Harry. Indeed, throughout a thousand years of history, kings and queens had been repeatedly threatened by renegade brothers, sisters or sons.

As Queen Camilla walked alone towards the abbey’s exit, Harry could not fail to notice her hands reach towards the loose crown, clearly fearful it might topple to the floor. Noticeably, Harry did not bow to the new Queen. Nor did anyone else in the family section.
Thirty-five minutes after the coronation ended, Harry was back at Heathrow. He had spent a mere twenty-nine hours in Britain. As the King and Queen stood on the Palace’s balcony waving at a vast

At the Windsor Castle concert that night, William included in his short speech a rebuke to the Sussexes: ‘Most importantly of all, my father has always understood that people of all faiths, all backgrounds and all communities deserve to be celebrated and supported. Pa, we are all so proud of you.’ There were loud cheers as William proclaimed, ‘God save the King.’
Ten days later, on 16th May 2023, Harry and Meghan were back in New York. Both appeared revitalised, not least to challenge the new King.

A few months after the Sussexes befriended and hosted in Montecito the celebrated feminist Gloria Steinem, the 89-year-old activist’s Ms Foundation for Women had acclaimed Meghan’s leadership with its World of Vision Award. The citation for Meghan’s prize celebrated ‘her global advocacy to empower and advocate on behalf of women and girls’, and she was praised as someone who ‘ignites policy and cultural change’. The award would be presented at a glittering celebration on the foundation’s fiftieth anniversary.

The Sussexes had every reason to welcome their return to the spotlight. Over the previous weeks, their approval ratings among Americans had fallen to about 25 per cent. While a minority of Americans still regarded the Sussexes as heroes, the majority had become exasperated by their attacks against the Royal Family. Meghan’s popularity had plunged 36 points to minus 33. After the publication of his book, Harry’s popularity had fallen 45 points to minus 22. The slide threatened to continue.

The sniping generated by South Park’s sarcastic cartoon was continued after the publication of Spare by television host Jimmy Kimmel mocking William’s and Harry’s grudge fight in the kitchen. New York Times switched sides. Under the headline ‘Has Prince Harry’s Confessional Tour Run Its Course?’ the newspaper asked whether the Sussexes’ rehash had become repetitive and tiresome.

Being spotted with Hollywood royalty would enhance her appearance in New York for the relaunch of an empowered woman with opinions.

In anticipation of the event, Steinem praised Meghan as ‘smart, funny and devoted to social issues’. Once he was spotted, the photographers’ interest soared. A joint photograph was worth thousands of dollars. The disappointed photographers agreed that the Sussexes had contrived to control the narrative – no photographs except on their terms.

Meghan’s power to attract publicity was critical to the charity. The ambition to raise $100 million over the following year depended on Meghan and Harry inspiring generosity from the one thousand high rollers in the ballroom.

They expected huge contributions over the following days. Instead, the night raised less than $1.5 million. One reason was the events that followed.

Aware of the court hearings in London, the Sussexes left the ballroom determined not only that their destination should remain unknown but also, on Harry’s orders, that they would not be photographed. Acting on Harry’s plan, as they emerged, bodyguards obscured their faces. ‘Security were being arseholes from the start,’ complained a photographer. ‘We’d waited for more than four hours and none of us could get shots.’

Yet soon after daybreak in New York, Harry’s version of the chase was dismissed by the New York Police Department (NYPD) and New York’s mayor, Eric Adams. The scenes, they agreed, were ‘challenging’, but it was ‘hard to believe’ there had been a two-hour chase in the congested city. ‘It was a bit chaotic,’ said the police spokesman, ‘but there were no collisions, injuries or threats.’

Photos of Meghan distributed by the celebrity news agency BackGrid showed her smiling from her SUV. According to BackGrid, Harry’s SUV was ‘reckless, zigzagging, coming close to other cars and blocking off streets. They pulled out into the oncoming lane and caused chaos.’

Mike Lombardi, the precinct’s police chief, was puzzled by Hansen’s claim. The Sussexes, he knew, never entered the police station. Nor did the Sussexes make a complaint or a request. And the police never went near Harry’s SUV. Police cars leaving the station for emergencies didn’t see Harry. The precinct chief declared that, ‘Harry and Meghan got carried away, the paparazzi got carried away, the media got carried away.’ All a single policeman posted outside the precinct station saw was the Sussexes getting into a yellow cab, only for it to be stuck stationary in the street. More confusion followed.

The cab driver, Sukhcharn Singh, recalled two cars following him. ‘I don’t think I would call it a chase,’ he recalled. ‘I never felt like I was in danger. They kept their distance.’ A garbage truck blocked his getaway cab. No photographer approached the stationary vehicle. After just ten minutes, Singh was back at the police station. He was paid $17 for the fare, plus a $50 tip. The Sussexes got back into the SUV and headed uptown alone to Jackson’s brownstone on the Upper East Side. Based on a briefing from Harry’s team, the New York Times misreported even that short journey: ‘The couple got home by police convoy without having their location discovered.’
Challenged that her version of a ‘near fatal, catastrophic car chase’ was clearly inaccurate, Hansen replied, ‘It would be abhorrent to suggest it was a PR stunt.’

With an eye to the live court proceedings in London, Harry demanded that the BackGrid photo agency surrender their photos. Clearly he had forgotten the ridicule he incited in 2021 after he condemned the First Amendment of the US Constitution, guaranteeing the freedom of the media. BackGrid’s lawyer repeated the mockery.
