Undeterred, Harry demanded an investigation into whether the police should have done more. The NYPD demurred. Harry called the Manhattan district attorney (DA). Impatiently, he demanded that the photographers be prosecuted. After interviewing two photographers and consulting the police officers at the 19th Precinct, the Manhattan DA decided that although the incident was ‘unfortunate’ and the journey was ‘stressful for the passengers’, the photographers were ‘unpleasant but not criminal’. Prince Harry and those speaking on his behalf were incorrect to say the ‘chase’ was ‘near catastrophic’, said the DA. It was ‘not a near-death experience’.

Under pressure from Harry, John Hart, the NYPD’s chief of intelligence, wrote on 18th September 2023 to Chief Superintendent Richard Smith of the Royal Family’s protection unit. Yet Hart’s letter could not have been what Harry expected. Hart confirmed that while the photographers’ conduct had been ‘reckless’ and ‘persistently dangerous’, the incident did not amount to an indictable offence. Nevertheless, Hart added, the NYPD ‘will be enhancing the security resources’ for the Sussexes, starting with a planned visit in October.

In London, the judge would decide that just because photographers were unpleasantly close did not mean that they had a hostile intention to kill Harry and Meghan. Once again Harry’s truthfulness was doubted.

The car chase wiped the awards ceremony off the news agenda. Instead of applause for the couple, there were sniggers and accusations. A-listers realised that having the Sussexes on their guest list posed a threat. Meghan’s publicists might leak details, distracting from the host, and possibly she would cause a scandal.

By now, it was clear that one of Meghan’s skills was to recognise bad news and seek to repair the damage. Philanthropy and ‘service’ was her solution. On 24th May, one week after the car chase, a tragic school

That day displayed an irony about the Sussexes’ world. Both claimed to have fled from Britain partly out of fear (unjustified in truth) that they and Archie would be denied police protection from assassination. Harry was making a huge effort to prove that he was vulnerable to potential murderers in London and needed British taxpayers to fund his protection. In America, both claimed they were safe. Yet the massacre in Uvalde, by no means a rare tragedy, exposed the real dangers of a country flooded by guns used frequently in random shootings.

On the day Meghan flew to Uvalde, Harry lost his claim in London to pay for his armed police protection. The government’s legal costs were £492,000. Generously, the judge ordered Harry to repay just £10,000. His second claim against the Home Office for free protection continued.

Harry did not apologise after arriving a day late in the London court-room, even after Judge Timothy Fancourt’s reprimand. Nothing, he said, could have prevented him celebrating Lilibet’s second birthday in Montecito.

Shortly after the hearing started on 6th June 2023, the first member of the Royal Family since Edward VII in 1891 stood in the witness box to testify against Mirror Group Newspapers (MGN). Four years after Harry and his lawyers had first accused the newspaper group of unlawful information gathering, Court 15 saw the result of thousands of hours of work costing millions of pounds. Dozens of boxes surrounded the barristers as David Sherborne stepped into the spotlight. The Mirror had not offered to pay enough compensation to stop the trial.
‘Call me Prince Harry,’ announced the 38-year-old. The judge ruled that Harry should be addressed as ‘Sir’.

Morland’s cartoon in The Times showed Harry placing his hand on a copy of Spare for the oath: ‘I swear to tell my truth, my whole truth and nothing but my truth.’ The Prince had good reason to be confident. MGN had already paid £105 million in compensation and costs to about six hundred claimants for hiring private investigators to hack into their voicemails or unlawfully obtain personal information. Mirror journalists

Harry claimed that the Mirror had spent £10.7 million commissioning 114 illegal investigations into his activities and relationships by hacking the mobile telephones of him, his friends and staff. Allegedly, 135 separate payments were made to private investigators searching Harry’s phone and 154 payments for hacking his friends, including Guy Pelly and Mark Dyer, described as Harry’s ‘second dad’, and his former nanny, Tiggy Legge-Bourke. ‘Utterly vile,’ Harry defined the Mirror’s journalists.

Discovering the Mirror’s illegality was time-consuming. His lawyers had looked for embarrassing stories about Harry in the Mirror Group’s newspapers, then searched for the journalists’ sources. First, they scoured the telephone records of the private investigators looking for calls to the mobile phones of Harry’s friends. Then they unearthed the Mirror’s payments to those private investigators who had hacked into voicemails. The lawyers did not know how much incriminating evidence had been destroyed, but the surviving records showed that Harry’s friends had been targeted.

‘A flood of illegality over a period of twenty years,’ Sherborne called it. He blamed Piers Morgan, the Mirror’s editor from 1995 until he was fired in 2004. Morgan, said Sherborne, ‘lies right at the heart of this in a number of ways’.Mirror stories cited by Harry – published in 2002 about Harry and drugs – appeared during Morgan’s tenure and he denied knowing about its source. He would be contradicted during the trial by Omid Scobie. Judge Fancourt accepted that Morgan had openly discussed phone hacking in front of Scobie. In the week after Omid Scobie was shown to be a liar about naming two Royal Family ‘racists’ in the first draft of his book Endgame (to be published later that year), the judge called Scobie a ‘straightforward

In his 55-page witness statement for the trial, Harry portrayed himself as an isolated and damaged target exploited by the media. ‘I have experienced hostility from the press since I was born,’ he said. Harry was particularly outraged that, when he was a young man, gangs of paparazzi had waited outside nightclubs swinging intimidating cameras: ‘It was like being swarmed by a rabid mob. Their arms stretched out pushing a button, taking hundreds of photographs all at once with the flash in your face. I couldn’t even see where I was going. As I reached the car, I could hear taunting. I was being egged on for a reaction, knowing I’d been out and had a few drinks.’ In his opinion, the tabloids coaxed a ‘damaged’ young man to excesses: ‘They took great pleasure in knocking me down, time and time again,’ he complained. Especially in their efforts to destroy his relationship with his girlfriend Chelsy Davy.

Blonde and vivacious, Davy had grown up on a game reserve near Victoria Falls in Zimbabwe. After meeting Harry as a teenager at the Berkshire Polo Club, their relationship was forged in Cape Town in 2005. They split up in 2011. He was twenty-seven and she was twenty-six. Harry blamed the tabloids’ hacking for ruining their relationship. Based on the voicemail messages on Davy’s phone, tabloid journalists had written juicy accounts of their stormy relationship.Harry said that at the time he did not realise the phones were being hacked. He assumed that friends and employees were betraying him to Mirror journalists. Not only were the newspaper reports ‘incredibly upsetting’, said Harry, but they also caused him ‘huge bouts of depression and paranoia’ because ‘he could not trust anybody’.

Based on his lawyers’ research, Harry listed incidents where he believed that the Mirror journalists could only have possessed the Mirror journalists and photographers had ‘booked into the hotel before we got there’; a surprise meeting with Davy arranged by his friend Mark Dyer at the El Remanso ranch in Lobos, Argentina, which was a secret even for Harry but not for the Mirror journalists; two stories headlined ‘Harry’s Girl to Dump Him’ and ‘Chelsy Is Not Happy’, reporting that ‘furious’ Davy had given Harry a ‘tongue-lashing down the phone’ after accusing him of flirting with a brunette at the party; and that Chelsy had made twelve phone calls in an hour and ‘begged [Harry] not to be a hero’ by going to Iraq; and then in an ‘emotional phone call’, Davy had asked for a trial separation.

After a private investigator had made six calls on a single day to Davy’s telephones to hear Harry’s voicemails, Mirror journalists reported that Harry had ‘bombarded her with texts and phone calls apologising for the antics that caused the split’ and had ‘repeatedly begged her’ to take him back.

The climax was the Sunday Mirror’s revelation on 25th January 2009 that Davy was ending the relationship.Under the headline, ‘HE just loves boozing & army SHE is fed up & is heading home’, the Mirror reported that Davy had ‘dumped’ the Prince because he loved the Army more than her. The final blast was published on 26th April 2009. Under the headline ‘Chelsy New Fella’, Harry was reportedly ‘bombarding’ Davy with calls asking her to take him back after she told him the truth – she had a new man.

Equally ‘shocking and so blatant’ for Harry were the Mirror’s reports allegedly based on private telephone conversations with his father. Expecting to be treated with deference, Harry was not prepared for cross-examination. He never expected his ‘truth’ to be challenged. Representing the Mirror, Andrew Green showed that with one exception, there was ‘no evidence whatsoever’ that the newspapers’ reports about Mirror stories were all legally sourced. Mirror journalists had copied stories published earlier in a rival newspaper, cited a BBC report, quoted Harry’s press spokesman or a Buckingham Palace statement, repeated Harry’s own interviews, secured an interview with Mark Dyer or relied on normal journalism – namely, speaking to a bar owner or an eyewitness.

Harry admitted that a Mirror description of his twelfth birthday could not have been produced by hacking because he did not own a mobile until the following year. With each confrontation exposing the fallacy of his claim, Harry replied, ‘I didn’t know,’ ‘I wasn’t aware’ or ‘I honestly can’t remember.’

At the end of five hours’ cross-examination on the first day, the Prince looked unhappy. ‘His accusations were vapid, unsubstantiated and some simply ridiculous,’ sniped the Sun.

Did Harry not realise, asked Green, that ‘Hooray’ referred to his class? For ‘Hooray’ to summarise a celebration would have required a comma after the word. Harry was stumped. ‘Your case’, Green told Harry, ‘is based on total speculation without any evidential basis whatsoever.’ Harry blinked. It’s likely that no one had ever spoken to him in public like that before. The common headline in the following day’s newspapers was ‘Total Speculation’. Those headlines, Harry admitted to Sherborne, were ‘even more destructive’. He added, ‘For my whole life, the press has misled me and covered up the wrongdoing.’ Sherborne would turn to the gallery and pronounce that the case was Harry’s campaign to reform the press.

Readers of newspaper accounts of Harry’s cross-examination would have believed that his chance of success was remote. But Sherborne understood the Prince’s advantages. The High Court had decided in 2015 that hacking was rampant in Mirror newspapers and no British judge had ever rejected a civil claim from a member of the Royal Family. Meghan’s successful case against the Mail on Sunday proved that England’s judges were prejudiced against the media, even though the Duchess had signed an inaccurate statement.

Pearl, the ‘heroic adventures’ of a twelve-year-old girl.

‘I could happily live here,’ Harry told his hosts.

Harry had first visited Lesotho in 2004 at the age of nineteen during his gap year between leaving Eton and entering Sandhurst. Two years later, he and Prince Seeiso of Lesotho co-founded the charity in honour of their mothers to help the one in four adults who were HIV positive in the nation, the world’s second highest rate of infection. Most sufferers were also victims of extreme poverty. Sentebale – which means forget-me-not in Sesotho – had survived early difficulties. In 2008, it emerged that just £84,000 of the £1.15 million raised through events, including a concert in memory of Diana and a TV documentary featuring Harry, had been spent on helping AIDS orphans. The charity’s first director had been paid £100,000 a year, plus the perks of a four-bedroom villa with staff and a private education for his three children. Locals in South Africa assumed that Harry was uninterested in the turmoil. Indeed, after arriving in Tokyo, it looked to be true.

Harry’s anger that day in Japan had been triggered by Haruhisa Handa’s loud executive director Midori Miyazaki. Appointed just weeks earlier, Chandauka, a Zimbabwean Oxford graduate and a senior associate at the global law firm Baker McKenzie, was flummoxed but not surprised by Harry’s irritation. Sentebale faced a watershed, she believed. For nearly twenty years, Harry had spoken about his devoted attention to Sentebale, but he had not visited Lesotho for four years and was unaware of the crisis.

Chandauka’s appointment had followed a search by headhunters to find a replacement for the British marketing executive Johnny Hornby, the chairman since 2018. Just weeks before the end of the financial year, Hornby had signalled his exit. One of Hornby’s complaints was Sentebale’s precarious finances. The annual expenditure – roughly £3.5 million – was never covered by its income from international agencies including UNICEF and rich foundations. Every year, the charity was running at an annual loss of about £370,000 and dependent on its reserves. The five staff in the London office cost £600,000 a year. Like the trustees, none of them visited Lesotho and added little to the charity’s operation other than to organise Harry’s two annual polo matches. Even the famous polo matches proved to be of limited value. Each match cost about £400,000 to stage and the profit from donations was only about £350,000, about 18 per cent of Sentebale’s income.

The only relief to the charity’s precarious finances had come in 2022 after Harry contributed £1.1 million from the profits of Spare. But making the charity dependent on Harry’s book disturbed pro-royalist donors. Some supporters had even resigned. And once Harry’s ‘tainted’ donation was spent, Hornby admitted he had no solution to finding more money in Britain. Rich Americans, he agreed, were the charity’s best hope, but he had no contacts.

Another potential casualty, Chandauka discovered, was Midori Miyazaki. She had exploited Harry in Tokyo because she knew Sentebale’s finances were dire and that Harry relied on Handa’s donation. But Miyazaki failed to take into account Harry’s moods. Harry, Chandauka discovered, hated talking about money. He expected Chandauka to resolve all the problems created by her predecessors.

At the outset, Harry praised Chandauka’s ‘experience, passion for social change and entrepreneurial spirit’. Between 2015 and 2019 she had sat on Sentebale’s board, and in 2021 had received an MBE for ‘extraordinary services’ to diversity in business. She had also advised a slew of public companies including Nike, Citibank and Alliance Boots. ‘She will bring’, Harry said, a ‘tremendous benefit to Sentebale’s next chapter.’

With that experience, Chandauka told Harry a harsh truth: Sentebale was struggling. Gleaming SUVs were parked outside its Lesotho headquarters, but some HIV children in the villages appeared to have been abandoned by Sentebale.

On their flight to Singapore, the outspoken lawyer introduced another unpleasant reality check. Corporate brands hate controversy. After conducting a brand audit among fifty organisations and donors, she told Harry she had discovered that his personal brand value had crashed. ‘People don’t want to be associated with your Netflix shows and especially not with Meghan,’ she reported. The survey also discovered that Meghan had little brand value in America. Across the globe, she actually damaged Harry’s remaining brand value.

Accustomed to being hailed as ‘box office’, Harry was stunned. Johnny Depp, he replied, had still attracted a lot of money despite his bitter courtroom battles with Amber Heard. Couldn’t he do the same? Times have changed, Chandauka replied. The proof was in the finances. Following critical comments about Harry and Meghan on the polo clubs’ websites and suspicions about Harry’s acceptance of a $1 million fee for addressing a conference in Florida organised by a controversial cryptocurrency firm,New York Post’s gossip column.

Sentebale, Harry told Chandauka, was relying too much on his money and his polo matches. He resented the pressure. He did not want to register that Spare and the Netflix series had damaged his brand value. And Nacho Figueras, added Chandauka, was hanging on to Harry to stem his own waning attraction.

By September 2023, Harry had understood Chandauka’s warning. At Sentebale’s board meeting, Harry agreed to close the charity’s London office, move the management to Lesotho and find more American donors. Most importantly, the charity would abandon its unique focus on HIV sufferers. Instead, under ‘Project Pivot’, Sentebale would promote children’s health and education. At the same meeting, the trustees considered Harry’s brand value. ‘The more you ascend, the less oxygen you have to tell the truth,’ Jonathan Mildenhall, a marketing expert introduced by Chandauka, told Harry. ‘Spare should have been called Duty,’ Mildenhall continued. ‘Your image is bad,’ the Prince was told. Raising money in America would be difficult because ‘you’re not special.’ ‘How come no one told me?’ asked Harry, puzzled by the term ‘brand audit’.

Meghan had been through the wringer enough to recognise that bad could get worse. The previous months had been plagued by indecision and problems. ‘Sir, your millions from Netflix for no one knows what.’

Ever since Virginia Giuffre, an American girl abused and trafficked by the convicted paedophile Jeffrey Epstein, filed a legal claim in 2014 in Florida against Andrew, Charles and his mother, Queen Elizabeth, had tried to believe Andrew’s denials of any connection to Giuffre.

Harry would not allow the High Court embarrassment to derail his quest to use the Invictus Games for his family’s return to Britain. But in choosing Birmingham, Harry had unwittingly exposed himself to unexpected controversy: the toxic reality of a city in turmoil, mired by threats of protest.

Among the twenty-two countries invited to compete in Birmingham was Israel. Inevitably, their small team would be drawn from former members of the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) injured fighting Hamas and Hezbollah in Gaza, Lebanon and Syria. In Vancouver, their 22-man squad had been warmly received and won thirteen medals, but the competitors had been asked not to parade their identity or flag outside the Convention Centre.

The challenge for Harry and the Invictus Games was existential. Would Birmingham’s Muslim activist community allow an Israeli team to peacefully compete in their city? Based on the Maccabi experience, that would be difficult to envisage.

The anticipated reception of Invictus in the UK in 2027 poses a challenge not only to Harry but also the King. Could the monarch allow himself to be embroiled in the inevitable protests, after the negative press the royal family had suffered over the preceding months and years? And more to the point, could the King allow himself to be influenced by his son, who still demanded an apology from his father, brother and sister-in-law? Considering all the damning words and lies spoken and written by the Sussexes against the Royal Family since 2021, and the enormous damage caused by the Sussexes’ exploitation of their royal titles, the chance of the King irritating the Prince and Princess of Wales to please the Sussexes is, in Spring 2026, remote.

Unexpectedly, Charles was paying the price for the delayed expulsion of his brother from his Windsor home and the Royal Family. Amid the fevered public debate, fundamental changes and transparency were demanded for the Royal Family’s finances and their status. Even normally moderate voices spoke of the King’s abdication, which would be a death sentence for the constitutional monarchy.

In Montecito, the Sussexes could take little comfort from Buckingham Palace’s turmoil. In Spare, Harry had scoffed about the unequal treatment of himself and ‘my uncle Andrew’: ‘Whatever grievances people had against us, sex crimes weren’t on the list.’
The feud embroiling the Royal family is far from resolution, and it remains to be seen what damage will be inflicted on an already fragile family.
